summary: A high‑energy 5/12/91 Shoreline show captured on a decent soundboard, highlighted by a scorching Deal and a smoking Help > Slip > Franklin’s.
review: The performance is consistently praised – the first set delivers one of the era’s best Deals and an unforgettable Help‑Slip‑Franklin’s sequence, while the second set offers a fiery Terrapin jam (despite a missing ending) and rare moments like Black‑Throated Wind. The soundboard recording is generally clear and balanced, though several listeners note low‑end excess, occasional glitches and the loss of the final minutes of the Terrapin.

ai_rating.rationale: Most reviewers give 4–5 stars and extol the musical performance; recording issues are mentioned but do not dominate, yielding a strong overall score.

recording_quality.technical_notes: Crisp instrument separation and clear mix overall; some listeners note excessive low‑end, occasional glitch in "C.C. Rider", and missing ~7 min of the Terrapin jam.

standout_songs: Help on the Way > Slipknot! > Franklin's Tower
poor_songs: Terrapin Station (missing ending, described as sloppy by one reviewer)
show_quality.setlist_flow: A classic 1991 Shoreline set: a tight first set capped by a blistering Deal, followed by a second set that builds through Help‑Slip‑Franklin’s into an expansive Terrapin jam and finishes with high‑energy rockers.

band_member_comments.Jerry: Repeatedly praised for his interplay with Bruce Hornsby and for delivering “tink tink” guitar magic throughout the show.
band_member_comments.Phil: Described as "absolutely wicked" on the second set, especially during Help and Slip; his bass drives the jam forward.
band_member_comments.Bob: Noted by at least one reviewer for being clearly audible in the mix, adding solid rhythm and vocals.
band_member_comments.Keys: Bruce Hornsby receives glowing remarks – “dynamite”, “crystal clear”, “smoking” – and is credited with elevating the entire performance.
band_member_comments.Drums: Bill Kreutzmann and Mickey Hart are generally praised for locking in, though a few comments mention excessive low‑end that may stem from drum capture.
